sectionTitle: 1.1 What is Chemistry: Study of Matter

core_concept.principle: Chemistry is the study of matter and the changes it undergoes.
core_concept.explanation: Everything around us is made of matter. Chemistry helps us understand what matter is, how it behaves, and how it interacts with energy.

image.alt: Collage of chemistry in daily life including cooking, medicine, and materials
image.caption: Chemistry is present in everything from the food we eat to the materials we use.

sections.introduction: Chemistry is often called the 'central science' because it connects physics with other natural sciences like biology and geology. It is the study of the composition, structure, properties, and reactions of matter.

introduction_image.alt: Diagram showing branches of chemistry like Organic, Inorganic, Physical
introduction_image.caption: Chemistry is divided into several specialized branches.

key_concept.definition: Chemistry is the scientific study of matter, its properties, how and why substances combine or separate to form other substances, and how substances interact with energy.
key_concept.context: This definition encompasses everything from the atoms inside a single cell to the vast reactions occurring in stars.

image.caption: Chemists use experiments to test hypotheses about matter.

sections.introduction: Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space (volume). If you can touch it, see it, or measure it, it is likely matter. Even things you cannot see, like air, are matter.

key_concept.definition: Matter is any substance that has mass and occupies space.
key_concept.context: Light, heat, and thoughts are NOT matter because they do not have mass or volume.

image.alt: Images of solid, liquid, and gas examples
image.caption: Solids, liquids, and gases are all forms of matter.

classifications.behavior: Maintains its shape regardless of the container.

examples.explanation: Water frozen into a solid state retains its cubic shape.

characteristics: Particles close but can slide past each other
classifications.behavior: Flows to fill the bottom of a container.

examples.explanation: Takes the shape of a cup or bottle but volume remains constant.
Flows freely but maintains specific volume.

characteristics: Particles far apart and move quickly
classifications.behavior: Spreads out to fill any available space.

examples.explanation: Steam expands to fill the kitchen when boiling water.
Air we breathe is a mixture of gases.

sections.introduction: We identify matter by observing its properties. These are divided into physical properties (observed without changing identity) and chemical properties (observed during chemical change).

categories.description: Characteristics that can be observed or measured without changing the substance's identity.
categories.detailed_mechanism: These involve observing the state, color, density, or melting point without creating new substances.

examples.explanation: Gold is yellow; copper is reddish-brown.
Mass per unit volume (e.g., lead is denser than foam).

categories.description: Characteristics that describe how a substance changes into a different substance.
categories.detailed_mechanism: These involve the substance's ability to undergo chemical reactions, such as burning, rusting, or rotting.

examples.explanation: The ability of a substance to burn in the presence of oxygen.
How readily a substance combines chemically with other substances.

image.caption: Iron reacting with oxygen to form rust.

sections.introduction: Matter is constantly changing. It is crucial to distinguish between physical changes (identity remains the same) and chemical changes (identity changes).

worked_examples.title: Identifying Physical vs. Chemical Changes
worked_examples.problem: Determine whether the following are physical or chemical changes: (a) Melting ice, (b) Burning wood, (c) Dissolving sugar in water.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze Melting Ice. Does the substance change identity? No, it is still H₂O, just in liquid form.
Analyze Burning Wood. Does the substance change identity? Yes, wood turns into ash, smoke, and gases.
Analyze Dissolving Sugar. Can the sugar be recovered? Yes, by evaporating the water. Identity remains sugar.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Physical changes do not alter chemical composition; chemical changes create new substances.

image.alt: Diagram comparing physical and chemical changes
image.caption: Visual comparison of change types.

summary: Chemistry is the study of matter and its changes. Matter is anything with mass and volume, existing primarily as solids, liquids, or gases. We describe matter using physical properties (like color and density) and chemical properties (like flammability). Changes in matter are classified as physical (identity stays the same) or chemical (new substances are formed).
